|--------|----------|----------|
| **Fab (Fragment antigen-binding)** | Antigen recognition | Two upper arms of the "Y" |
| **Fc (Fragment crystallizable)** | Effector function, FcRn binding, half-life | Stem of the "Y" |
| **Variable region (V)** | Specificity-determining | Tips of Fab |
| **Constant region (C)** | Class/isotype determination | Body of Fab + entire Fc |
| **CDR (Complementarity-Determining Regions)** | Direct antigen contact | 3 per V domain (H3 is most diverse) |
| **Hinge region** | Flexibility between Fab and Fc | Between Fab arms and Fc |

Each chain has **variable (V)** and **constant (C)** domains:
- Heavy chain: V_H, C_H1, C_H2, C_H3
- Light chain: V_L, C_L (kappa κ or lambda λ)

---

## 1.5 Classes (Isotypes) of Antibodies

| Isotype | Heavy Chain | Approx. Serum Conc. | Key Feature | Crosses Placenta? |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **IgG** | γ | **~75% of total Ig** (8–18 mg/mL) | Most abundant, long half-life | Yes (via FcRn) |
| **IgM** | μ | ~10% | Pentamer (5 units), first responder | No |
| **IgA** | α | ~15% | Dimer in secretions, mucosal immunity | No |
| **IgD** | δ | <1% | B-cell receptor marker | No |
| **IgE** | ε | <1% | Allergic response, anti-parasitic | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU:** Therapeutic mAbs are **predominantly IgG (especially IgG1)** because of its long serum half-life (~21 days) and well-characterized effector functions (ADCC, CDC).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hybridoma Technology — The Core Production Method

Hybridoma technology, developed by **Köhler and Milstein (1975, Nature, 256: 495–497)**, is the **classical method** for producing monoclonal antibodies. It fuses antibody-producing B-lymphocytes with immortal myeloma cells.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Immunization of the host** (typically BALB/c mouse):
   - Inject the target antigen (with adjuvant) into the mouse 2–4 times over several weeks.
   - This stimulates a polyclonal B-cell response in the spleen → B-lymphocytes secrete the desired antibody.

2. **Extraction of B-lymphocytes**:
   - Sacrifice the immunized mouse → remove spleen → isolate B-lymphocytes from splenocytes.
   - These B-cells *can* produce the antigen-specific antibody **but have a finite lifespan** in culture (die after a few days).

3. **Cell fusion (Hybridoma creation)**:
   - Mix splenocytes with **immortal myeloma cells** (e.g., SP2/0, NS0, P3X63Ag8.653 lines).
   - Add **polyethylene glycol (PEG, ~50% w/v)** — a fusogen that transiently merges plasma membranes.
   - Alternatively, use **electrofusion** (short electric pulses).
   - **Why both cell types?**
     - B-lymphocytes → antibody specificity, but mortal
     - Myeloma cells → immortality (rapid division), but cannot make antibodies
     - **Hybridoma** = *specificity* + *immortality* = "biological immortal antibody factory"

4. **Selection via HAT medium**:
   - **H**ypoxanthine, **A**minopterin, **T**hymidine medium.
   - **Aminopterin blocks the de novo nucleotide synthesis pathway** → cells must use the **salvage pathway** requiring **hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT)**.
   - Myeloma cells used are **HGPRT-deficient** → die in HAT.
   - Unfused B-cells die naturally (short lifespan).
   - **Only hybridomas (with B-cell HGPRT + myeloma immortality) survive.**

5. **Screening for desired antibody (ELISA / Immunofluorescence)**:
   - Supernatants from hybridoma clones are tested for the desired antigen-specific antibody.
   - Positive clones are identified.

6. **Single-cell cloning (limiting dilution)**:
   - Dilute cells to statistically one cell/well → grow clonal populations.
   - Re-screen → ensure **monoclonality** (all cells descended from a single progenitor).

7. **Expansion and cryopreservation**:
   - Clone is grown in bulk culture or in vivo (ascites in mice) for large-scale antibody production.
   - Master cell bank (MCB) is created and cryopreserved for GMP consistency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **HAT selection** is the *critical* step. Examiners frequently ask: *"Why is HAT medium used and what does it select for?"* — Answer: selects for **HGPRT⁺ hybridomas**; eliminates unfused myeloma (HGPRT⁻) and unfused B-cells (mortal).

---

## 2.2 Modern Recombinant Production Methods

Classical hybridomas (mouse-derived) are now complemented or replaced by **recombinant DNA techniques**:

| Method | Host System | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|--------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Hybridoma (mouse)** | Murine hybridoma cells | Classical, well-established | Murine antibodies → HAMA response in humans |
| **Phage display** | *E. coli* + M13 phage | No animals needed, fully in vitro | Selection bias, may need affinity maturation |
| **Transgenic mice (XenoMouse)** | Mice with human Ig loci | Produces **fully human antibodies** | Expensive, ethical concerns |
| **Single B-cell cloning** | Sorted human B-cells + RT-PCR | Natural human pairing | Low throughput, requires donor |
| **CHO cell expression** | Chinese Hamster Ovary cells | Industry gold standard, scalable, PTMs | Expired infrastructure |
| **Yeast / Plant expression** | *Pichia pastoris*, tobacco | Cheap, scalable | Glycosylation differences |

---

## 2.3 Humanization of Monoclonal Antibodies

Therapeutic mAbs evolved through **four generations** to reduce immunogenicity in humans:

| Generation | Composition | Human Content | Example Drug |
|------------|-------------|---------------|--------------|
| **Murine** ("-omab") | 100% mouse protein | 0% | Muromonab-CD3 (OKT3) |
| **Chimeric** ("-ximab") | Mouse V regions + Human C regions | ~65–70% | Rituximab, Infliximab |
| **Humanized** ("-zumab") | Mouse CDRs grafted onto human framework | ~90–95% | Trastuzumab, Bevacizumab |
| **Fully Human** ("-umab") | Entirely human sequence (transgenic mice / phage display) | ~100% | Adalimumab, Pembrolizumab |

> [!NOTE]
> **Suffix nomenclature** (WHO INN system) is examinable:
> - **-omab** = murine
> - **-ximab** = chimeric
> - **-zumab** = humanized
> - **-umab** = fully human
> - Subclass tags: **-ci(r)-** = cardiovascular, **-li(m)-** = immune, **-tu(m)-** = tumor

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Sheet — Monoclonal Antibodies**

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|-------------------|------------|-------------|
| **Affinity (Kd)** | $K_d = \dfrac{[Ab][Ag]}{[Ab\text{-}Ag]}$ | Dissociation constant; lower = higher affinity |
| **Functional Affinity (Avidity)** | $K_{av} = f(K_d, n)$ | Combined strength of both Fab sites (bivalent) |
| **Molecular Weight (IgG)** | $M \approx 150\ \text{kDa}$ | Standard size for IgG1, IgG2, IgG3, IgG4 |
| **Half-life (IgG, FcRn-mediated)** | $t_{1/2} \approx 21\ \text{days}$ | Due to pH-dependent FcRn recycling |
| **Antibody-Antigen Binding (Langmuir)** | $\dfrac{B}{B_{max}} = \dfrac{[Ag]}{K_d + [Ag]}$ | Sigmoidal saturation curve |
| **Stoichiometry (IgG : Ag)** | 1 IgG : 2 Ag (epitope) | Bivalent binding |
| **Linker Math (ADC)** | $\text{DAR} = \dfrac{\text{Drug molecules}}{\text{Antibody}}$ | Drug-to-Antibody Ratio (typically 3–4) |
| **Tm (melting temp, Fab)** | $T_m \approx 65\text{–}75\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Thermal stability indicator |
| **Size for nanocarrier compatibility** | $d \approx 10\ \text{nm}$ | Fab tip-to-tip dimension (~10–14 nm) |

### 2.4.1 Why Kd Matters (Bionanotechnology Context)

In nanobiosensors, the **limit of detection (LOD)** is governed by antibody affinity:

$$\text{LOD} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{K_a}} = \sqrt{K_d}$$

A mAb with **Kd = 10⁻¹⁰ M** can detect antigens 1000× more sensitively than a pAb with Kd = 10⁻⁷ M. This is why **mAbs dominate diagnostic immunoassays** (ELISA, lateral flow, SPR, electrochemical sensors).

---

## 2.5 Mechanisms of Action (Therapeutic mAbs)

Therapeutic mAbs function via **multiple mechanisms**, often overlapping:

1. **Neutralization** — block pathogen/toxin receptor binding
2. **Antibody-Dependent Cellular Cytotoxicity (ADCC)** — Fc engages NK cell FcγRIIIa (CD16) → target lysis
3. **Complement-Dependent Cytotoxicity (CDC)** — C1q binds Fc → membrane attack complex
4. **Antibody-Dependent Cellular Phagocytosis (ADCP)** — macrophages engulf opsonized cells
5. **Receptor blockade/agonism** — block or activate signaling receptors (e.g., HER2, PD-1, EGFR)
6. **Payload delivery** — Antibody-Drug Conjugates (ADCs), radioimmunotherapy

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Clinical Medicine** | Cancer (Rituximab, Trastuzumab), autoimmune (Adalimumab), COVID-19 (Sotrovimab) |
| **Diagnostics** | Pregnancy tests (anti-hCG), infectious disease rapid tests, tumor marker detection |
| **Nanosensors** | Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR), Field-Effect Transistor (FET) biosensors |
| **Drug Delivery (Bionano)** | ADCs, immunoliposomes, antibody-targeted nanoparticles |
| **Imaging** | PET/SPECT imaging with radiolabeled mAbs (e.g., ⁸⁹Zr-Trastuzumab) |
| **Affinity Chromatography** | Protein A/G columns purify mAbs via Fc binding |
| **Agriculture** | Lateral flow strip tests for pathogens/pesticides |
| **Research** | Western blot, flow cytometry, immunoprecipitation, ChIP |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Reality:** The global mAb market exceeded **USD 200 billion in 2023**, making it the largest biopharmaceutical class. CHO cell bioreactors can produce **>5 g/L** of mAb in fed-batch culture.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equilibrium Binding of a Monoclonal Antibody to its Antigen

This derivation connects antibody-ligand binding to **sensor sensitivity** — a high-yield KTU analytical question.

### Step 1: Define the Binding Reaction

The bivalent IgG antibody has **two identical Fab binding sites** (for a monovalent antigen epitope). For simplicity, consider the **first Fab arm binding**:

$$Ab + Ag \;\rightleftharpoons\; Ab\text{-}Ag$$

### Step 2: Write the Equilibrium Constant

The intrinsic association constant $K_a$ (units: M⁻¹) is:

$$K_a = \dfrac{[Ab\text{-}Ag]}{[Ab][Ag]}$$

The dissociation constant:

$$K_d = \dfrac{1}{K_a} = \dfrac{[Ab][Ag]}{[Ab\text{-}Ag]}$$

### Step 3: Define Total Antibody Concentration

$$[Ab]_0 = [Ab] + [Ab\text{-}Ag]$$

Solving for free antibody:

$$[Ab] = [Ab]_0 - [Ab\text{-}Ag]$$

### Step 4: Express Bound Fraction (B/Bmax)

Substituting into the $K_d$ expression:

$$K_d = \dfrac{([Ab]_0 - [Ab\text{-}Ag])\,[Ag]}{[Ab\text{-}Ag]}$$

Rearranging:

$$K_d \cdot [Ab\text{-}Ag] = [Ab]_0 \cdot [Ag] - [Ab\text{-}Ag]\cdot[Ag]$$

$$[Ab\text{-}Ag]\,(K_d + [Ag]) = [Ab]_0 \cdot [Ag]$$

$$\dfrac{[Ab\text{-}Ag]}{[Ab]_0} = \dfrac{[Ag]}{K_d + [Ag]}$$

Since $[Ab\text{-}Ag]_{max} = [Ab]_0$ (saturating antigen), the **fractional occupancy** is:

$$\boxed{\;\theta = \dfrac{B}{B_{max}} = \dfrac{[Ag]}{K_d + [Ag]}\;}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the **Langmuir isotherm** for antibody binding. When $[Ag] = K_d$, half the sites are occupied.

### Step 5: Numerical Illustration for KTU

Suppose an IgG1 mAb has $K_d = 10^{-9}\ \text{M}$ (= 1 nM) for a tumor antigen. Calculate the fractional occupancy at $[Ag] = 5\ \text{nM}$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-9}}{10^{-9} + 5 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{5}{6} = 0.833 = 83.3\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** When the antigen concentration is **10× the Kd**, occupancy ≈ 91%. Diagnostic sensors should be designed such that the antigen range of interest is **near or above Kd** for maximum sensitivity.

### Step 6: Extension to Bivalent (Avidity) Effect

For the **bivalent IgG**, the second Fab can bind a second epitope. The functional affinity (avidity) is amplified. If the second binding is treated as independent with similar $K_d$, the effective $K_{d,eff}$ for a bivalent antibody is **lower** (tighter) than monovalent:

$$K_{d,eff} \approx \dfrac{K_d^{\,2}}{C}$$

where $C$ is a constant related to the **effective concentration** of the second epitope near the first Fab (geometry-dependent; ~10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁹ M for typical Fab flexibility).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why bivalent mAbs matter:** A monovalent Fab (e.g., in a Fab'-only ADC) loses ~10–100× functional affinity compared to full IgG. This is why **most therapeutic mAbs remain bivalent IgG**, except in special constructs (e.g., bispecifics like blinatumomab, which is ~55 kDa BiTE).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How mAbs Compare to Alternative Recognition Elements

This matrix is critical for **KTU Part B questions on choosing mAbs vs alternatives in nanobiosensing**.

| Property | Monoclonal IgG (~150 kDa) | Polyclonal IgG (~150 kDa) | scFv (~25 kDa) | Nanobody (~15 kDa) | Aptamer (~6–25 kDa) |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Specificity** | High (1 epitope) | Moderate (multi-epitope) | High | High | High |
| **Affinity (Kd)** | 10⁻⁷–10⁻¹⁰ M | 10⁻⁶–10⁻⁹ M | 10⁻⁶–10⁻¹⁰ M | 10⁻⁷–10⁻¹⁰ M | 10⁻⁷–10⁻¹² M |
| **Size** | ~14 nm (Y-shape) | ~14 nm | ~3 nm | ~2.5 × 4 nm (smallest Ig) | ~2 nm |
| **Tissue penetration** | Moderate | Moderate | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Production cost** | High (CHO cells) | Lower (animal sera) | Moderate (E. coli) | Moderate (yeast) | Low (chemical synth.) |
| **Stability** | Moderate (cold chain) | Moderate | Good | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Immunogenicity (human)** | Low–moderate | High | Low | Very low (human VH-only) | None |
| **Fc effector function** | Yes | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Half-life in vivo** | ~21 days | Variable | ~hours–days | ~hours–days | ~minutes–hours |
| **Best use** | Therapeutics, diagnostics | Research reagents | Imaging, fusion constructs | Modular nanobio tools | Synthetic nanobiosensors |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** When asked to design a **nanocarrier for crossing the blood-brain barrier (BBB)**, a **nanobody or scFv** is preferred over full IgG because of superior tissue penetration and lower molecular weight.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem: ADC Stoichiometry

**Problem (typical KTU Part B style):** A monoclonal antibody (150 kDa) is conjugated to a cytotoxic drug (MW = 700 Da) via lysine residues. After purification, **3.5 drug molecules are attached per antibody on average**. Calculate the (a) **DAR** (Drug-to-Antibody Ratio) and (b) the **% mass contributed by the drug** in the ADC.

### Solution

**Part (a):** DAR is given directly:
$$\text{DAR} = \dfrac{3.5\ \text{drug molecules}}{1\ \text{antibody}} = 3.5$$

**Part (b):** Mass of 1 ADC = antibody + DAR × drug mass:
$$\text{Mass}_{\text{ADC}} = 150{,}000\ \text{Da} + (3.5 \times 700)\ \text{Da}$$
$$\text{Mass}_{\text{ADC}} = 150{,}000 + 2{,}450 = 152{,}450\ \text{Da}$$

Drug mass fraction:
$$\%\,\text{Drug} = \dfrac{2{,}450}{152{,}450} \times 100\% = 1.61\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students forget to multiply DAR by drug MW. Common error: using only 700/150,000 = 0.47% (the mass of *one* drug per antibody), forgetting DAR = 3.5. The correct answer is 1.61%.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Why IgG Half-Life is ~21 Days (FcRn-Mediated Recycling)

**Conceptual derivation (not a full kinetic model, but a clean logical flow):**

1. IgG is internalized by endothelial cells into endosomes (pH ~6.0).
2. At acidic pH, the **neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn)** binds the Fc region with high affinity.
3. FcRn-bound IgG is **recycled back to the cell surface**, avoiding lysosomal degradation.
4. At physiological pH 7.4 in blood, IgG dissociates from FcRn → re-enters circulation.
5. Unbound IgG (in lysosome) is degraded → contributes to baseline clearance.

This recycling pathway **extends half-life from ~hours to ~21 days**. Engineering strategies that enhance FcRn binding (e.g., **M252Y/S254T/T256E = "YTE" mutation**) can extend half-life to **>2 months** — used in next-generation mAbs like ravulizumab.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Antibody Structure (Y-shaped Glycoprotein)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Monoclonal Antibody IgG, 150 kDa] --> B[Fab Region x2, Antigen Binding]
    A --> C[Fc Region, Effector Function]
    A --> D[Hinge Region, Flexibility]

    B --> B1[Variable Heavy Domain VH]
    B --> B2[Variable Light Domain VL]
    B --> B3[CDR Loops, 3 per V domain]
    B --> B4[Constant Heavy 1, CH1]
    B --> B5[Constant Light, CL, kappa or lambda]

    C --> C1[CH2 Domain, C1q binding for CDC]
    C --> C2[CH3 Domain, FcRn binding for half-life]
    C --> C3[CH2 to CH3, FcgammaR binding for ADCC]

    D --> D1[Disulfide bonds, 2 inter-heavy]
    D --> D2[Threonine-rich flexible linker]

    B3 --> E[Antigen Epitope, single specific site]
    C1 --> F[Complement C1q]
    C2 --> G[Neonatal Fc Receptor, FcRn]
    C3 --> H[NK cell FcgammaRIIIa, CD16]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** The `Y` shape is built from the graph. Notice the parallel between **B (Fab) → Antigen binding** and **C (Fc) → Effector function** — this dual function is the central design principle of mAbs.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Hybridoma Technology Production Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Immunize BALB/c mouse with target antigen + adjuvant] --> S2[Step 2: Harvest spleen, isolate B-lymphocytes]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Fuse with HGPRT-deficient myeloma cells using PEG 1500 or electrofusion]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Plate in HAT selection medium]
    S4 --> S5{Step 5: Surviving colonies are hybridomas, B-cell HGPRT + myeloma immortality}
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Screen supernatants by ELISA for antigen-specific antibody]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Identify positive wells, perform limiting dilution cloning]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Single-cell clones verified monoclonality, expand and cryopreserve]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Produce mAb in vivo ascites or in vitro bioreactor]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Purify via Protein A/G affinity chromatography]

    S5 --> D1[Unfused B-cells: die, mortal]
    S5 --> D2[Unfused myeloma: die, HGPRT-negative, blocked by aminopterin]
    S5 --> D3[B-B cell or myeloma-myeloma fusions: rare, can be screened out]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Humanization Generations of Therapeutic mAbs

```mermaid
graph LR
    M0[Generation 0: Murine mAb, 0 percent human, suffix -omab, OKT3] --> M1[Generation 1: Chimeric mAb, 65 to 70 percent human, suffix -ximab, Rituximab, Infliximab]
    M1 --> M2[Generation 2: Humanized mAb, 90 to 95 percent human, CDR grafted, suffix -zumab, Trastuzumab, Bevacizumab]
    M2 --> M3[Generation 3: Fully Human mAb, 100 percent human, suffix -umab, Adalimumab, Pembrolizumab, via XenoMouse or phage display]

    M0 --> I1[Problem: HAMA, Human Anti-Mouse Antibody response, rapid clearance, allergic reactions]
    I1 --> M1
    M1 --> I2[Problem: Still ~30 percent murine V-region, residual immunogenicity]
    I2 --> M2
    M2 --> I3[Problem: CDR grafting may reduce affinity, requires back-mutation engineering]
    I3 --> M3
    M3 --> I4[Current gold standard: minimal immunogenicity, long half-life, scalable in CHO cells]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block 4 — Functional Architecture: mAb in a Bionanotechnology Application (ADC with Tumor Targeting)

```mermaid
graph TD
    T[Tumor Cell, overexpresses HER2 receptor] --> R[HER2 Antigen on membrane]
    A[mAb Trastuzumab, anti-HER2 IgG1] --> FAB1[Fab 1 binds HER2 epitope 1]
    A --> FAB2[Fab 2 binds HER2 epitope 2, bivalent]
    A --> FC[Fc engages NK cell FcgammaRIIIa, ADCC]
    A --> ADC[Payload: DM1 maytansine conjugated to lysine, DAR equals 3.5]

    FAB1 --> S1[Step A: Specific binding to HER2-positive tumor]
    FAB2 --> S1
    FC --> S2[Step B: NK cell-mediated lysis]
    ADC --> S3[Step C: Internalization, lysosomal cleavage of MCC linker, DM1 released]
    S3 --> S4[Step D: DM1 binds tubulin, inhibits microtubule polymerization]
    S4 --> S5[Step E: G2/M cell cycle arrest, apoptosis]
    S1 --> OUT[Therapeutic outcome: tumor regression]
    S2 --> OUT
    S5 --> OUT

    O[Off-target cells, HER2-low or negative] -.->|no binding| N[Minimal toxicity]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block 5 — Decision Flow: Choosing mAb Format in a Nanobiosensor

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Analyte size?} -->|Small molecule, <1 kDa| P1[Use competitive immunoassay, mAb paired with hapten conjugate]
    Q1 -->|Protein, >5 kDa| P2[Use sandwich immunoassay, capture mAb + detection mAb, different epitopes]
    Q1 -->|Cell or virus| P3[Use whole-cell ELISA or lateral flow, single mAb]

    P2 --> Q2{Environment?}
    Q2 -->|In vivo, e.g., imaging| F1[Full IgG, 21-day half-life, Fc effector optional]
    Q2 -->|In vitro point-of-care| F2[scFv or Fab fragment, smaller, faster kinetics]
    Q2 -->|Nanosensor on chip| F3[Intact IgG via Protein A immobilization, oriented binding]

    F1 --> U1[Trade-off: long half-life vs tissue penetration]
    F2 --> U2[Trade-off: lower affinity vs superior diffusion]
    F3 --> U3[Trade-off: oriented vs random immobilization, ~2x higher activity when oriented via Protein A]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Define monoclonal antibodies. How are they different from polyclonal antibodies?**

**Model Answer:**

Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are **homogeneous immunoglobulin molecules** derived from a **single clone of B-lymphocytes (or hybridoma)**, which are therefore **identical in structure, specificity, and affinity**, and recognize **a single epitope** on an antigen.

**Difference from polyclonal antibodies:**

| Feature | Monoclonal Antibody | Polyclonal Antibody |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Source | One B-cell clone | Many B-cell clones |
| Epitopes recognized | One (single epitope) | Multiple |
| Homogeneity | Homogeneous | Heterogeneous |
| Specificity | High | Broader |
| Cross-reactivity | Low | Higher |
| Production | Hybridoma technology (in vitro) | Animal immunization (in vivo) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Batch-to-batch consistency | High | Variable |
| Use | Therapeutics, diagnostics | Research, general assays |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for tabulated comparison)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**What is hybridoma technology? Name the key reagents used in HAT selection and state their functions.**

**Model Answer:**

Hybridoma technology is a method developed by **Köhler and Milstein (1975)** to produce monoclonal antibodies. It involves the **fusion of antibody-secreting B-lymphocytes (mortal, antigen-specific) with myeloma cells (immortal, antibody-deficient)** to create **hybridoma cells** that combine the property of antibody specificity with immortality.

**HAT selection medium components:**

| Reagent | Function |
|---------|----------|
| **H**ypoxanthine | Substrate for the salvage pathway enzyme HGPRT |
| **A**minopterin | Blocks **de novo** nucleotide synthesis pathway |
| **T**hymidine | Substrate for thymidine kinase in salvage pathway |

**Mechanism:** Aminopterin blocks the de novo pathway, forcing cells to use the salvage pathway. Unfused myeloma cells lack **hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (HGPRT)** and cannot use hypoxanthine → **die**. Unfused B-cells die naturally (mortal). **Only hybridomas (with B-cell-derived HGPRT) survive** in HAT medium.

**(3 marks: 1 for hybridoma definition + 1 for HAT components + 1 for selection logic)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the structure of a typical IgG monoclonal antibody with a labeled diagram. Discuss the role of the Fab and Fc regions. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the principle, procedure, and applications of hybridoma technology for the production of monoclonal antibodies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Structure of IgG (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** A monoclonal IgG antibody is a Y-shaped glycoprotein of **molecular mass ~150 kDa**, consisting of **2 identical heavy chains (H, ~50 kDa each)** and **2 identical light chains (L, ~25 kDa each)**, held together by **disulfide bonds** and non-covalent interactions.

**Structure Description (4 marks):**

- **Heavy chain (H):** Has 4 domains — 1 variable (V_H) and 3 constant (C_H1, C_H2, C_H3).
- **Light chain (L):** Has 2 domains — 1 variable (V_L) and 1 constant (C_L). Light chains are either **kappa (κ)** or **lambda (λ)**, never both.
- **Variable region (V):** Located at the tips of the Fab arms. Contains 3 **complementarity-determining regions (CDRs)** in each V domain. CDR-H3 is the **most diverse** and most often contacts antigen.
- **Constant region (C):** Provides the structural framework and isotype-specific effector functions.
- **Hinge region:** Located between C_H1 and C_H2, contains **proline-rich sequences** allowing flexibility (Fab arms can move ~60° relative to Fc).
- **Disulfide bonds:** 2 inter-heavy (in the hinge), 1 between each H and L chain, multiple intrachain.

**Roles of Fab and Fc (2 marks):**

- **Fab (Fragment antigen-binding):** Contains V_H, V_L, C_H1, C_L. **Function:** Binds antigen with high specificity. The affinity of a single Fab for antigen is **monovalent** ($K_d \sim 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-9}$ M). Two Fabs per IgG give **bivalency → avidity enhancement**.
- **Fc (Fragment crystallizable):** Contains C_H2 and C_H3. **Functions:**
  1. **Effector recruitment:** binds **Fcγ receptors (FcγRI, II, III)** on immune cells → ADCC, ADCP.
  2. **Complement activation:** binds **C1q** at the C_H2 domain → CDC.
  3. **Half-life regulation:** binds **neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn)** at acidic endosomal pH → recycled → **t₁/₂ ≈ 21 days** in serum.
  4. **Placental transfer** (in pregnancy): FcRn-mediated.
  5. **Protein A/G purification** in downstream processing.

**Diagram (essential for full marks):** Draw and label the Y-shaped IgG with H, L, V, C, CDR, Fab, Fc, hinge.

### Part (b) — Hybridoma Technology (7 marks)

**Principle (2 marks):** Hybridoma technology exploits cell fusion to combine the **antibody specificity of B-lymphocytes** with the **immortality of myeloma cells**. The fused hybrid cell (hybridoma) is then selected using **HAT medium** and cloned to produce **monoclonal antibodies**.

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Immunization** of BALB/c mouse with antigen + adjuvant (2–4 doses over 3–4 weeks). → B-cells in spleen produce desired antibody.
2. **Cell harvest & fusion:** Splenocytes fused with HGPRT-deficient myeloma (SP2/0, NS0) using **50% PEG 1500** (or electrofusion).
3. **HAT selection:** Cultured in **Hypoxanthine-Aminopterin-Thymidine** medium.
   - Aminopterin blocks de novo nucleotide synthesis.
   - Unfused myeloma cells lack HGPRT → cannot use salvage pathway → **die**.
   - Unfused B-cells → **die** (mortal, ~5–7 days in culture).
   - **Only B-cell × myeloma hybridomas survive** (B-cell provides HGPRT, myeloma provides immortality).
4. **Screening** by ELISA/immunofluorescence to identify clones producing the antigen-specific mAb.
5. **Cloning by limiting dilution** to ensure monoclonality (one cell per well → expand).
6. **Expansion & Production** either in vitro (bioreactor) or in vivo (mouse ascites). Purify via **Protein A/G affinity chromatography**.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Diagnostic kits (HIV, hepatitis, COVID-19 lateral flow tests)
- Therapeutics (cancer, autoimmune, infectious diseases)
- Research reagents (Western blot, flow cytometry, IP, IHC)
- Nanosensor biorecognition elements (SPR, FET, electrochemical)
- Targeted drug delivery (ADCs, immunoliposomes)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q. (a)** What are the different generations of therapeutic monoclonal antibodies? Compare them in terms of human content and immunogenicity. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the role of monoclonal antibodies in bionanotechnology. Discuss the design of an antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) as a targeted nanotherapeutic. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Generations of mAbs (7 marks)

| Generation | Composition | Human Content | Suffix | Example | Year |
|------------|-------------|---------------|--------|---------|------|
| **Murine** | 100% mouse sequence | 0% | -omab | Muromonab (OKT3) | 1986 |
| **Chimeric** | Mouse V regions fused to human C regions | ~65–70% | -ximab | Rituximab, Infliximab | 1997 |
| **Humanized** | Mouse CDRs grafted into human framework; rest human | ~90–95% | -zumab | Trastuzumab, Bevacizumab | 1998 |
| **Fully Human** | Entirely human sequence (transgenic mice/phage display) | ~100% | -umab | Adalimumab, Pembrolizumab | 2002+ |

**Comparison of Immunogenicity (3 marks):**

- **Murine mAbs:** Strongest immunogenicity → **HAMA (Human Anti-Mouse Antibody) response** → rapid clearance (t₁/₂ ~ 1–2 days), allergic reactions, reduced efficacy on repeated dosing. OKT3 is no longer in use due to this.
- **Chimeric mAbs:** ~30% mouse V-region still triggers **HACA (Human Anti-Chimeric Antibody)** response, but reduced.
- **Humanized mAbs:** ~5–10% mouse (CDRs) → minimal immunogenicity; **CDR grafting** may reduce affinity, requiring **back-mutations** to restore binding. Approved drugs like Trastuzumab are well-tolerated.
- **Fully human mAbs:** Near-zero immunogenicity → preferred for chronic indications (e.g., Adalimumab for rheumatoid arthritis, dosed weekly/biweekly).

**Naming Convention (1 mark):** WHO International Nonproprietary Name (INN) system — suffix indicates origin (-omab, -ximab, -zumab, -umab) and target (sub-stem).

### Part (b) — mAbs in Bionanotechnology + ADC Design (7 marks)

**Role of mAbs in Bionanotechnology (3 marks):**

Monoclonal antibodies serve as the **molecular recognition module** of various bionanotechnology platforms:
1. **Nanosensors:** mAbs immobilized on gold nanoparticles, quantum dots, graphene FETs, or SPR chips — achieve ultra-sensitive detection (e.g., cardiac troponin I detection at pg/mL).
2. **Targeted drug delivery:** mAbs conjugated to nanocarriers (liposomes, polymeric NPs, dendrimers, exosomes) → active targeting of cancer cells with reduced off-target toxicity.
3. **Immunoimaging:** mAbs labeled with radioisotopes (⁸⁹Zr, ⁶⁴Cu) for PET imaging of tumors.
4. **Theranostics:** combined diagnostic + therapeutic function in one construct.
5. **Affinity purification:** mAb-conjugated magnetic beads for biomarker isolation.

**ADC Design (4 marks):**

An **Antibody-Drug Conjugate (ADC)** is a tripartite nanotherapeutic comprising:

1. **Antibody (mAb):** Provides **specificity** — targets a tumor-associated antigen (e.g., HER2, CD30, Trop-2). Usually humanized or fully human IgG1.
2. **Cytotoxic payload (drug):** Highly potent (often sub-nanomolar IC₅₀). Examples:
   - **DM1/DM4** (maytansine derivatives, tubulin inhibitor)
   - **MMAE/MMAF** (auristatins, tubulin inhibitor)
   - **SN-38, DXd** (topoisomerase I inhibitors)
3. **Linker:** Connects drug to antibody. Two types:
   - **Cleavable** (cathepsin-B, acid, GSH-sensitive) → releases drug in lysosome
   - **Non-cleavable** (SMCC, thioether) → requires full antibody degradation

**Design Parameters:**
- **DAR (Drug-to-Antibody Ratio):** typically **3–4** (optimal balance between potency and PK).
- **Conjugation chemistry:** Lysine (random) or cysteine (site-specific, e.g., THIOMAB™).
- **Linker stability in plasma:** must survive circulation to avoid off-target release.

**Examples:** T-DM1 (Trastuzumab + DM1, HER2⁺ breast cancer), Brentuximab vedotin (anti-CD30 + MMAE, Hodgkin lymphoma), Trastuzumab deruxtecan (anti-HER2 + DXd, gastric cancer).

**Mechanism:** (1) mAb binds antigen on tumor cell → (2) internalization → (3) lysosomal cleavage of linker → (4) drug released → (5) drug binds its intracellular target (microtubule or topoisomerase) → (6) cell cycle arrest / apoptosis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks in Monoclonal Antibody questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HAT and HT medium.** HAT includes **Aminopterin** (the de novo blocker); HT is just for feeding salvage pathway. Many students write "HT medium selects hybridomas" — this is **wrong**; it is **HAT** that selects.
>
> 2. **Forgetting that HGPRT is in the B-cell, not the myeloma.** The HGPRT-deficient phenotype is the **myeloma's** defect. The hybridoma is rescued by the B-cell's HGPRT gene. Writing "myeloma provides HGPRT" will lose marks.
>
> 3. **Mixing up Fab and Fc functions.** Fab = **antigen binding** (specificity). Fc = **effector function + half-life** (via FcγR, C1q, FcRn). These are NOT interchangeable. A common error: "Fab binds C1q" — **false**; C1q binds Fc.
>
> 4. **Wrong nomenclature suffix.** Murine = -omab, chimeric = -ximab, humanized = -zumab, fully human = -umab. Mixing these up loses marks in Part A definitions.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to mention HAT selection as a 2-step process.** HAT = selection + screening. Selection (HAT eliminates unfused cells) and screening (ELISA for antigen-specific clones) are **separate** steps.
>
> 6. **Numerical error in ADC DAR calculation.** Use DAR = drug molecules per antibody, not the mass ratio unless explicitly asked.
>
> 7. **Missing the diagram.** A labeled IgG diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in Part B structure questions. Skipping the diagram = at least 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density KTU Rapid-Revision Summary — Monoclonal Antibodies**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Definition:** mAb = homogeneous antibody from a single B-cell clone → binds **one epitope**
- [x] **Structure:** 2H + 2L, **150 kDa**; Fab (V) + Fc (C); Hinge; CDRs (3 per V)
- [x] **5 Isotypes:** IgG, IgM, IgA, IgD, IgE — therapeutic mAbs are **IgG (esp. IgG1)**
- [x] **Half-life:** **~21 days** (IgG, FcRn-mediated recycling)

### Hybridoma Technology (Köhler & Milstein, 1975)
- [x] **Mouse immunization** → splenocytes (B-cells) **+** HGPRT⁻ myeloma → **PEG fusion**
- [x] **HAT selection** → only **B × myeloma hybridomas** survive
- [x] **ELISA screening** → **limiting dilution cloning** → **monoclonal** cell line
- [x] **Production:** in vitro (bioreactor, CHO cells) or in vivo (mouse ascites) → **Protein A purification**

### Humanization Generations
- [x] **Murine (-omab)** → **Chimeric (-ximab)** → **Humanized (-zumab)** → **Fully Human (-umab)**
- [x] Human content: 0% → 65–70% → 90–95% → 100%
- [x] Modern mAbs = fully human IgG1 produced in **CHO cells**

### Therapeutic Mechanisms
- [x] **Neutralization, ADCC (FcγR), CDC (C1q), ADCP, Receptor blockade, ADC payload delivery**

### Bionanotechnology Applications
- [x] **Nanosensors** (SPR, FET, lateral flow) — mAb as recognition element
- [x] **ADCs** — mAb + linker + cytotoxic drug; **DAR ~ 3–4**
- [x] **Immunoliposomes, immunonanoparticles** for targeted drug delivery
- [x] **Imaging** — radiolabeled mAbs for PET/SPECT

### Key Equations to Memorize
- [x] $K_d = \dfrac{[Ab][Ag]}{[Ab\text{-}Ag]}$ (lower Kd = higher affinity)
- [x] $\theta = \dfrac{[Ag]}{K_d + [Ag]}$ (Langmuir isotherm for mAb binding)
- [x] $M_{IgG} \approx 150\ \text{kDa}$ (standard mass)
- [x] $t_{1/2}^{IgG} \approx 21\ \text{days}$ (FcRn recycling)
- [x] $\text{DAR} = \dfrac{\text{Drug molecules}}{\text{Antibody}}$ (typical = 3–4)

### Common Drug Examples (must recognize)
- [x] **Rituximab** (anti-CD20, lymphoma)
- [x] **Trastuzumab** (anti-HER2, breast cancer; basis of T-DM1 ADC)
- [x] **Adalimumab** (anti-TNF-α, rheumatoid arthritis, fully human)
- [x] **Bevacizumab** (anti-VEGF, cancer, humanized)
- [x] **Pembrolizumab** (anti-PD-1, immune checkpoint, fully human)
- [x] **Muromonab-CD3** (anti-CD3, first mAb, murine, now withdrawn)

### Examiner's Mantra
> [!NOTE]
> **Always include in any mAb answer:**
> 1. **A labeled IgG diagram** (Y-shaped, Fab + Fc + H + L + V + C + CDR)
> 2. **HAT selection mechanism** (aminopterin + HGPRT logic)
> 3. **Nomenclature suffix** (-omab/-ximab/-zumab/-umab)
> 4. **Numerical specifications** (150 kDa, 21 days, DAR)
> 5. **One application from therapeutics + one from bionanotechnology**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tools in Nanobiotechnology: Microscopy and Imaging Techniques such as SEM, AFM, and STM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT523 - INTRODUCTION TO BIONANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tools in Nanobiotechnology: Microscopy and Imaging Techniques such as SEM, AFM, and STM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Microscopy & Imaging in Nanobiotechnology?

In the context of **Bionanotechnology**, microscopy and imaging techniques refer to the family of analytical instruments capable of visualizing, characterizing, and manipulating structures at the **nanoscale (1–100 nm)** — the operational regime of biomolecules such as proteins, DNA, lipid membranes, viruses, and engineered nanocarriers (liposomes, polymeric NPs, quantum dots).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> "Nanobiotechnology imaging tools are high-resolution analytical instruments that use electron beams, sharp physical probes, or quantum tunneling currents to generate topographical, morphological, and electronic maps of biological and synthetic nanostructures with sub-nanometer precision."

The three flagship techniques mandated by the KTU 2024 PEEBT523 Module 3 syllabus are:

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Probe Used |
|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **SEM** | Scanning Electron Microscopy | Focused electron beam |
| **AFM** | Atomic Force Microscopy | Mechanical sharp tip (cantilever) |
| **STM** | Scanning Tunneling Microscopy | Quantum tunneling electrons |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need these tools:** The human eye resolves ~0.1 mm. Optical microscopes reach ~200 nm (limited by light wavelength). To "see" a DNA strand (2 nm wide) or a protein fold, we must either:
> (a) Use a probe with a smaller wavelength (electrons → SEM), or
> (b) Physically "feel" the surface (AFM), or
> (c) Exploit quantum effects (STM).

### 🔬 Analogy 1 — SEM: "The Blind Man's Electron Cane"
Imagine a blind man (the detector) walking across a textured surface (your sample) using a long, thin cane (the electron beam). The cane taps the surface at millions of points in a precise grid; the strength and pattern of echoes (secondary electrons) reveal the bumps, pits, and edges — producing a 3D topographical map. The "blind man" cannot see atoms, but he can feel the *shape* with extraordinary precision.

### 🔬 Analogy 2 — AFM: "The Fingerprint Reader of Atoms"
Think of a very sharp stylus mounted on a flexible spring, similar to the needle of a vinyl record player (LP). As the stylus rides over the grooves of a record (your sample), the spring bends up and down. A laser aimed at the back of the spring records every deflection, building a 3D relief of the surface — but at the scale of **individual atoms**.

### 🔬 Analogy 3 — STM: "The Quantum Whistle Between Two Mountains"
Picture two sharp mountain peaks separated by a deep, dark valley. A whistle blown on one peak can be heard faintly on the other — even though sound waves *cannot* cross the valley. This is **quantum tunneling**: electrons "leak" across a tiny vacuum gap. By measuring the loudness (tunneling current), we deduce the exact gap width — atom by atom.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Snapshot (Critical for KTU Board Exam)

| Parameter | SEM | AFM | STM |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Probe | Electron beam | Mechanical tip | Conducting tip + bias |
| Sample type | Conductive (coated if not) | Any (insulator OK) | Must be **conductive** |
| Environment | High vacuum | Air, liquid, vacuum | Ultra-high vacuum (mostly) |
| Resolution (lateral) | **1–10 nm** | **0.1–1 nm** | **0.01–0.1 nm (atomic)** |
| Resolution (vertical) | — | **0.01 nm** | **0.001 nm** |
| Imaging in liquid? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (biological) | ❌ Rare |
| Typical biological use | Cell morphology, virus structure | Live biomolecules, DNA, proteins | Atomic lattices, conductive biomaterials |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** AFM is the *only* technique among the three that can image biological samples (proteins, DNA) in their **native aqueous environment** — making it the most bionano-relevant tool in this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SEM — Scanning Electron Microscopy

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle (Why & How)

1. **Electron Generation:** A **thermionic emitter** (tungsten filament, LaB₆ crystal) or **field emission gun (FEG)** produces electrons via heating or a strong electric field.
2. **Acceleration:** Electrons are accelerated through a potential difference (typically **0.5–30 kV**), gaining kinetic energy $E = eV$.
3. **Focusing:** A set of **electromagnetic lenses** (condenser + objective) demagnify the electron source to a probe of diameter **~1–10 nm** at the sample surface.
4. **Scanning:** **Scan coils** raster the focused beam across the sample in a TV-like pattern (line-by-line).
5. **Signal Generation:** Beam–sample interaction generates:
   - **Secondary electrons (SE)** — topographical contrast (low energy, <50 eV)
   - **Backscattered electrons (BSE)** — compositional (Z) contrast
   - **Characteristic X-rays** — elemental analysis (EDS/EDX)
6. **Detection:** A **Everhart–Thornley detector** converts SE/BSE into photons, then to electrical signal.
7. **Image Formation:** The signal intensity at each (x, y) scan position is mapped to pixel brightness on a monitor.

> **Engineering Insight:** SEM resolution is fundamentally limited by **electron wavelength** and **spherical aberration** of electromagnetic lenses. Lowering wavelength (higher V) and using FEGs improves resolution.

---

### 2.1.2 Resolution Criterion (Why)

The smallest resolvable feature $d$ is given by the **Abbe-like criterion** adapted for electron optics:

$$d \;=\; 0.6\,\lambda / \sin\alpha \;\approx\; 0.6\,\lambda / \beta$$

where $\beta$ is the convergence semi-angle of the probe-forming lens.

---

## 2.2 AFM — Atomic Force Microscopy

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

1. A microfabricated **cantilever** (typically Si or Si₃N₄) with an integrated sharp tip (radius **~2–10 nm**) is brought close to the sample.
2. **Tip–sample interaction forces** ($F_{ts}$) — primarily **van der Waals**, but also electrostatic, capillary, chemical, and Pauli repulsion — deflect the cantilever according to **Hooke's law**.
3. A **laser beam** reflected off the cantilever's back is detected by a **position-sensitive photodiode (PSPD)**, converting nanometer deflections into voltage.
4. A **piezoelectric scanner** (tube or stack) translates the sample (or tip) in (x, y, z) with sub-Ångstrom precision.
5. Feedback electronics maintain a constant setpoint (force, amplitude, or frequency) to generate the image.

### 2.2.2 Three Operating Modes

| Mode | Tip–Sample Distance | Force Regime | Best For |
|------|---------------------|--------------|----------|
| **Contact** | < 0.5 nm | Repulsive (10⁻⁸–10⁻⁶ N) | High resolution, hard samples |
| **Tapping (Intermittent)** | 1–2 nm (oscillating) | Attractive + Repulsive (cyclic) | Soft biological samples |
| **Non-contact** | 2–10 nm | Attractive (van der Waals) | Atomic resolution, ultra-soft |

> **Bionano Insight:** **Tapping mode** is the workhorse for biomolecules — it minimizes lateral shear forces that would otherwise sweep proteins or DNA off the substrate.

---

### 2.2.3 Cantilever Mechanics (Why the math matters)

The cantilever behaves as a **linear Hookean spring**. Deflection $\Delta z$ produces force $F$:

$$F \;=\; -k\,\Delta z$$

The **spring constant** $k$ of a rectangular cantilever is:

$$k \;=\; \frac{E\,w\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of cantilever material (Pa)
- $w$ = width (m)
- $t$ = thickness (m)
- $L$ = length (m)

The **resonant frequency** (important for tapping mode) is:

$$f_{0} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\frac{k}{m^{*}}} \;=\; \frac{\alpha_{n}^{2}}{2\pi L^{2}}\,\sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A}}$$

where $\alpha_{1} \approx 1.875$ for the fundamental flexural mode, $I = wt^{3}/12$, and $m^{*}$ is the effective mass.

---

## 2.3 STM — Scanning Tunneling Microscopy

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle (Quantum Tunneling)

1. An atomically sharp **conducting tip** (typically W or Pt-Ir) is positioned ~**0.3–1 nm** above a conducting sample.
2. A **bias voltage** $V_b$ (typically **10 mV – 1 V**) is applied between tip and sample.
3. Electrons **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through the vacuum barrier, producing a **tunneling current** $I_t$.
4. $I_t$ depends **exponentially** on the tip–sample distance $d$ — making STM exquisitely sensitive to vertical topography.

### 2.3.2 The Tunneling Current (Derivation Logic)

By solving the 1D Schrödinger equation for an electron crossing a rectangular potential barrier of height $\phi$ (work function) and width $d$ (vacuum gap), the wavefunction decays as:

$$\psi(d) \;=\; \psi(0)\,e^{-\kappa d}$$

The probability current (∝ $|\psi|^2$) gives the tunneling current:

$$I_{t} \;=\; I_{0}\,\exp(-\kappa d)$$

with the **decay constant**:

$$\kappa \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,m_{e}\,\phi}}{\hbar}$$

Therefore:

$$I_{t} \;=\; I_{0}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{\sqrt{2\,m_{e}\,\phi}}{\hbar}\,d\right)$$

> **Why this matters for imaging:** A change of $\Delta d = 0.1$ nm in tip height changes $I_t$ by roughly an order of magnitude (since $\phi \approx 4$ eV ⇒ $\kappa \approx 1.025$ Å⁻¹). Hence STM achieves **vertical resolution of 0.001 nm**.

### 2.3.3 Imaging Modes

- **Constant-Current Mode (CCM):** Feedback adjusts tip height to keep $I_t$ constant. Tip trace = topograph. Most common; slow but accurate.
- **Constant-Height Mode (CHM):** Tip height is fixed; $I_t$ varies with topography. Fast, but risks tip crash on rough samples.

---

| 2 | Electron wavelength (relativistic) | $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_e e V (1 + eV/2m_e c^{2})}$ | Required for V > 50 kV |
| 3 | SEM resolution (Abbe) | $d \approx 0.6\,\lambda / \beta$ | $\beta$ = convergence angle |
| 4 | De Broglie wavelength | $\lambda = h / p$ | h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s |
| 5 | AFM Hooke's law | $F = -k\,\Delta z$ | N when k (N/m), Δz (m) |
| 6 | Cantilever spring constant | $k = E w t^{3} / (4 L^{3})$ | Rectangular geometry |
| 7 | Cantilever resonant freq. | $f_{0} = (\alpha_{1}^{2} / 2\pi L^{2}) \sqrt{EI / \rho A}$ | $\alpha_{1} = 1.875$ |
| 8 | STM tunneling current | $I_{t} = I_{0} \exp(-\kappa d)$ | A |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications in Bionanotechnology

| Application | Preferred Tool | Why |
|-------------|----------------|-----|
| Imaging viruses (e.g., SARS-CoV-2, ~100 nm) | SEM | 3D surface morphology, large fields of view |
| Imaging live protein folding | AFM (tapping, in liquid) | Native aqueous environment, real-time |
| Imaging individual DNA bases | STM (in UHV) or AFM | Sub-nm resolution needed |
| Mapping mechanical stiffness of cancer cells | AFM (Force Spectroscopy) | Quantitative Young's modulus |
| Characterizing drug-delivery nanoparticles (size, shape) | SEM + AFM | Morphology + size validation |
| Verifying surface functionalization of biosensors | AFM + SEM | Coverage, roughness, defects |
| Imaging graphene defects for bioelectronics | STM | Atomic precision, electronic structure |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Electron Wavelength (SEM Resolution Foundation)

**Goal:** Express the electron wavelength $\lambda$ as a function of accelerating voltage $V$ (non-relativistic and relativistic cases).

### Non-Relativistic Case (V < ~10 kV)

**Step 1:** A free electron accelerated through potential $V$ gains kinetic energy equal to the work done on it:

$$E_{k} \;=\; e\,V$$

**Step 2:** From the kinetic energy, the electron momentum is:

$$p \;=\; \sqrt{2 m_{e} E_{k}} \;=\; \sqrt{2\,m_{e}\,e\,V}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **de Broglie relation** $\lambda = h / p$:

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{h}{\sqrt{2\,m_{e}\,e\,V}}$$

**Step 4:** Plug in constants:
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $m_{e} = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**Step 5:** Evaluate the prefactor:

$$\frac{h}{\sqrt{2\,m_{e}\,e}} \;=\; \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}$$

The denominator:
$$\sqrt{2 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;=\; \sqrt{2.918 \times 10^{-49}} \;=\; 5.402 \times 10^{-25}$$

So the prefactor = $6.626 \times 10^{-34} / 5.402 \times 10^{-25} = 1.227 \times 10^{-9}$ m·V$^{1/2}$.

$$\boxed{\lambda \;=\; \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}}\;\text{nm}}$$

> **Numerical example:** For $V = 20{,}000$ V, $\lambda = 1.227 / \sqrt{20000} = 1.227 / 141.4 = 0.00868$ nm = **8.7 pm**.

### Relativistic Case (V > ~50 kV)

**Step 1:** The relativistic energy is:

$$E_{k} \;=\; (\gamma - 1)\,m_{e} c^{2} \;=\; e\,V$$

where $\gamma = 1 / \sqrt{1 - v^{2}/c^{2}}$.

**Step 2:** The relativistic momentum is:

$$p \;=\; \sqrt{2\,m_{e}\,e\,V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2\,m_{e} c^{2}}\right)}$$

**Step 3:** Apply de Broglie:

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{h}{\sqrt{2\,m_{e}\,e\,V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2\,m_{e} c^{2}}\right)}}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate for $V = 100$ kV:
- $eV = 1.602 \times 10^{-14}$ J
- $m_e c^2 = 8.187 \times 10^{-14}$ J
- $1 + eV/(2m_e c^2) = 1 + 0.0978 = 1.0978$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{-9}}{\sqrt{1.0978 \times 10^{5}}} = \frac{1.227 \times 10^{-9}}{331.3} = 3.70 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m} = \textbf{3.7 pm}$$

> **Real-world value:** This is ~100,000× shorter than visible light (550 nm), explaining why electron microscopes can resolve atomic-scale features.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — STM Tunneling Current (1D Quantum Model)

**Goal:** Derive $I_{t} = I_{0} \exp(-\kappa d)$ from the Schrödinger equation.

### Step 1: Set Up the Potential Barrier

For a simple model, the tip–vacuum–sample junction is approximated as a **rectangular potential barrier** of height $\phi$ (≈ work function) and width $d$:

$$U(x) \;=\; \begin{cases} 0, & x < 0 \quad \text{(tip metal)} \\ \phi, & 0 \leq x \leq d \quad \text{(vacuum)} \\ 0, & x > d \quad \text{(sample metal)} \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Write the Time-Independent Schrödinger Equation

In the barrier region ($0 \leq x \leq d$):

$$-\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2 m_{e}}\,\frac{d^{2}\psi}{d x^{2}} + \phi\,\psi \;=\; E\,\psi$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^{2}\psi}{d x^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2 m_{e}(\phi - E)}{\hbar^{2}}\,\psi$$

### Step 3: Define the Decay Constant

Define:

$$\kappa^{2} \;=\; \frac{2 m_{e}(\phi - E)}{\hbar^{2}}$$

For $E < \phi$ (electrons below the barrier), $\kappa^{2} > 0$, and the equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^{2}\psi}{d x^{2}} \;=\; \kappa^{2}\,\psi$$

### Step 4: Solve the ODE

The general decaying solution is:

$$\psi(x) \;=\; A\,e^{-\kappa x} \;+\; B\,e^{+\kappa x}$$

For a thick barrier ($d \gg 1/\kappa$), the growing term is unphysical, so:

$$\psi(x) \;=\; A\,e^{-\kappa x}$$

### Step 5: Tunneling Probability

The probability density in the barrier is:

$$\vert \psi(x) \vert^{2} \;=\; \vert A \vert^{2}\,e^{-2 \kappa x}$$

The transmission probability across the barrier of width $d$ is approximately:

$$T \;\approx\; e^{-2 \kappa d}$$

### Step 6: Relate to Tunneling Current

The tunneling current is proportional to the transmission coefficient:

$$I_{t} \;\propto\; T \;\approx\; e^{-2 \kappa d}$$

Combining all constants into $I_0$:

$$\boxed{I_{t} \;=\; I_{0}\,\exp(-2 \kappa d) \;\approx\; I_{0}\,\exp(-\kappa d) \;\text{(engineering form)}}$$

where the engineering collapse uses an effective $\kappa$ that absorbs the factor of 2.

> **Numerical example:** $\phi = 4$ eV, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.
> Convert $\phi$: $4 \text{ eV} = 4 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 6.408 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
> $\kappa = \sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 6.408 \times 10^{-19}} / 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$
> $= \sqrt{1.167 \times 10^{-49}} / 1.055 \times 10^{-34} = 1.080 \times 10^{-24.5} / 1.055 \times 10^{-34} \approx 1.025 \times 10^{10}$ m⁻¹ = **1.025 Å⁻¹**.

> So a **0.1 nm** change in $d$ alters $I_t$ by a factor $e^{0.1025 \times 0.1 \times 10^{10}} = e^{1.025} \approx 2.8$ → **~3× current change for 1 Å vertical motion** ⇒ atomic-scale vertical sensitivity.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — AFM Cantilever Spring Constant

**Goal:** Justify $k = E w t^{3} / (4 L^{3})$ for a rectangular cantilever.

### Step 1: Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation

For a slender beam undergoing small transverse deflection $z(x)$, the governing ODE is:

$$E I \,\frac{d^{4} z(x)}{d x^{4}} \;=\; q(x)$$

where $q(x)$ is the distributed load (force per unit length) and $I$ is the area moment of inertia.

### Step 2: Cantilever Boundary Conditions

For a cantilever of length $L$ fixed at $x = 0$ and free at $x = L$, with a **point load** $F$ applied at the tip:

$$z(0) = 0, \quad z'(0) = 0, \quad M(L) = -EI z''(L) = 0, \quad V(L) = -EI z'''(L) = F$$

### Step 3: Solve for Deflection

Solving the ODE with the above BCs gives the tip deflection:

$$\Delta z(L) \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I}$$

### Step 4: Identify the Spring Constant

By definition, $F = k \cdot \Delta z$, so:

$$k \;=\; \frac{F}{\Delta z} \;=\; \frac{3\,E\,I}{L^{3}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute $I$ for Rectangular Cross-Section

For a rectangular cross-section of width $w$ and thickness $t$:

$$I \;=\; \frac{w\,t^{3}}{12}$$

Substituting:

$$k \;=\; \frac{3\,E}{L^{3}} \cdot \frac{w\,t^{3}}{12} \;=\; \frac{E\,w\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{k \;=\; \frac{E\,w\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}}}$$

> **Numerical example:** A typical AFM cantilever: $L = 200$ µm, $w = 30$ µm, $t = 2$ µm, $E$ (Si) = 130 GPa.
> $k = (130 \times 10^{9})(30 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^{3} / [4 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}]$
> $= (130 \times 10^{9})(30 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18}) / [4 \times 8 \times 10^{-15}]$
> $= 3.12 \times 10^{-11} / 3.2 \times 10^{-14} = 0.975$ N/m ≈ **1 N/m** (typical soft cantilever).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Microscope (Decision Flowchart Reasoning)

> **KTU 2024 Board Style:** Examiners love comparative tables. This is the definitive one for Module 3.

| Decision Criterion | SEM | AFM | STM |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Is the sample conductive?** | Required (or coat with Au/Pt) | Any type | **Required** |
| **Is the sample in aqueous buffer?** | No | **Yes (key advantage)** | No |
| **Atomic resolution required?** | No (max ~1 nm) | **Yes (sub-nm vertical)** | **Yes (atomic)** |
| **Sample is live biomolecule?** | No (vacuum kills cells) | **Yes (tapping in liquid)** | No |
| **Need force / mechanical data?** | No | **Yes (force spectroscopy)** | No |
| **Large-area survey (µm–mm)?** | **Yes** | Yes (slow) | No (atomic scale) |
| **Cost (relative)?** | Medium | **Low** | High |
| **Sample preparation effort?** | High (dehydration, coating) | Minimal | High (clean surfaces) |
| **Typical biological specimen** | Cells, tissue, viruses, NPs | DNA, proteins, membranes, live cells | Conductive biomaterials, metalloproteins |
| **Inventor (KTU loves history!)** | Knoll & Ruska (1931, TEM); SEM by Zworykin (1942) | Binnig, Quate, Gerber (1986) | **Binnig & Rohrer (1981, Nobel 1986)** |

## 4.1 SEM — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Electron Source: Tungsten Filament or FEG] --> A2[Accelerating Voltage 0.5 to 30 kV]
    A2 --> A3[Condenser Lens 1: First Demagnification]
    A3 --> A4[Condenser Lens 2: Further Demagnification]
    A4 --> A5[Objective Lens: Final Focus to 1 to 10 nm Spot]
    A5 --> A6[Scan Coils: Raster Pattern X Y]
    A6 --> A7[Sample Stage: Beam Sample Interaction]
    A7 --> A8[Signal Generation: SE, BSE, X rays]
    A8 --> A9[Everhart Thornley Detector]
    A9 --> A10[Amplifier and Signal Processing]
    A10 --> A11[Display: 2D Topographic Map]
    A7 -.X-ray.-> A12[EDS Detector: Elemental Analysis]

    style A1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style A7 fill:#98FB98
    style A11 fill:#87CEEB

```

**Block-Level Functional Reading:**
- **Top path (A1→A11):** Topographic + morphological imaging
- **Side path (A7→A12):** Compositional/elemental analysis
- The **scan coils** are what makes it a *scanning* microscope (as opposed to TEM which uses broad-beam transmission).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Sample Mounted on Piezo Scanner XYZ] --> B2[Sharp Tip Radius 2 to 10 nm on Cantilever]
    B2 --> B3[Tip Sample Interaction: VdW, Electrostatic, Capillary]
    B3 --> B4[Cantilever Deflection Delta z]
    B4 --> B5[Laser Diode Illuminates Cantilever Back]
    B5 --> B6[Position Sensitive Photodiode PSPD: 4 Quadrants]
    B6 --> B7[Differential Signal: Vertical and Lateral]
    B7 --> B8[Feedback Controller: Maintains Setpoint]
    B8 --> B9[Piezo Z Adjustment Signal]
    B9 --> B1
    B8 --> B10[Image Reconstruction Computer]
    B10 --> B11[3D Topographic Map]

    B12[Mode Selector: Contact, Tapping, Non Contact] --> B8

    style B2 fill:#FFB6C1

    style B11 fill:#87CEEB
    style B12 fill:#DDA0DD
```

**Block-Level Functional Reading:**
- The **feedback loop** (B8→B9→B1) is the heart of AFM — it keeps the tip–sample force constant to prevent damage, especially for soft biomolecules.
- The **PSPD** converts nanometer-scale tip deflections into measurable voltage via optical lever amplification (typically **1000× gain**).

---

## 4.3 STM — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Bias Voltage Source: 10 mV to 1 V] --> C2[Conducting Tip W or PtIr]
    C2 --> C3[Vacuum Gap d approx 0.3 to 1 nm]
    C3 --> C4[Conducting Sample Substrate]
    C4 --> C5[Electrons Tunnel Across Barrier]
    C5 --> C6[Tunneling Current It: 0.1 to 10 nA]
    C6 --> C7[Transimpedance Amplifier: Converts I to V]
    C7 --> C8[Feedback Controller: Maintains It or d]
    C8 --> C9[Piezo Z Adjustment]
    C9 --> C2
    C8 --> C10[Image Reconstruction: It x y or d x y]
    C10 --> C11[Atomic Resolution Topograph]

    C12[Mode Selector: Constant Current CCM or Constant Height CHM] --> C8
    C13[Variable d_z: Records Local Density of States LDOS] --> C10

    style C3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style C6 fill:#98FB98
    style C11 fill:#87CEEB
    style C12 fill:#DDA0DD
```

**Block-Level Functional Reading:**
- The **exponential sensitivity** of $I_t$ to $d$ is the fundamental quantum-mechanical basis of STM.
- The **feedback loop** (C8→C9→C2) operates at kHz–MHz bandwidth — much faster than AFM, allowing atomic-resolution real-time imaging.
