KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define nano carriers. List the major classes of nano carriers with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Nano carriers** are nanoscopic vehicles (1–1000 nm, typically 10–200 nm for biomedical use) engineered to encapsulate, carry, and deliver therapeutic or diagnostic agents (drugs, genes, proteins, vaccines) to target sites with improved efficacy, controlled release, and reduced toxicity. **Major classes with examples:** 1. **Lipid-based:** Liposomes — e.g., Doxil® (PEGylated liposomal doxorubicin). 2. **Polymer-based:** PLGA nanoparticles — e.g., biodegradable polymeric nanospheres. 3. **Inorganic:** Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) — e.g., photothermal therapy agents. 4. **Viral-based:** Adeno-Associated Virus (AAV) vectors — gene therapy. 5. **Hybrid / Theranostic:** Au-coated SPIONs — combined imaging + therapy. **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 4 classes with examples)** --- ### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: What is the EPR effect? Why is it important in nano carrier-based drug delivery?** **Model Answer:** The **EPR (Enhanced Permeability and Retention) effect** is a phenomenon observed in solid tumors wherein: - Tumor vasculature is **leaky** (gap junctions of 200–2000 nm) due to abnormal angiogenesis. - Tumor tissues have **poor lymphatic drainage**. Together, these features allow **nano carriers (10–200 nm)** to selectively extravasate into tumor interstitium and **accumulate** there for prolonged periods, while being excluded from normal tissues with tight endothelial junctions. **Importance in nano carrier drug delivery:** - Enables **passive targeting** without surface ligand functionalization. - Increases local drug concentration at tumor site (10–50× higher). - Reduces systemic toxicity and off-target effects. - Forms the rationale for why nano carrier sizes are designed in the **10–200 nm** window. **(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for significance)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Classify nano carriers with a detailed account of **lipid-based nano carriers** (liposomes, SLN, NLC). Discuss their structure, methods of preparation, and applications in drug delivery. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **EPR effect** and compare **passive and active targeting** strategies in nano carrier-based drug delivery. Discuss the role of **PEGylation** in nano carrier design. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) Lipid-Based Nano Carriers (7 marks) **1. Classification context (1 mark):** Nano carriers are classified into lipid-based, polymer-based, inorganic, viral, and hybrid systems based on their material composition. **2. Liposomes (2 marks):** - **Structure:** Spherical vesicles with one or more phospholipid bilayers surrounding an aqueous core. Phospholipids (e.g., phosphatidylcholine, cholesterol) self-assemble in aqueous media. - **Drug loading:** Hydrophilic drugs trapped in aqueous core; hydrophobic drugs incorporated in the lipid bilayer. - **Preparation methods:** - **Thin-film hydration:** Lipids dissolved in organic solvent, evaporated to form thin film, hydrated with aqueous buffer → multilamellar vesicles (MLVs). - **Sonication / Extrusion:** MLVs reduced to small unilamellar vesicles (SUVs) of uniform size. - **Reverse-phase evaporation:** Produces large unilamellar vesicles (LUVs) with high encapsulation efficiency. - **Applications:** Doxil® (cancer), AmBisome® (antifungal), Onpattro® (siRNA delivery for hereditary amyloidosis). **3. Solid Lipid Nanoparticles (SLN) (2 marks):** - **Structure:** Solid lipid core (triglycerides, fatty acids, waxes) stabilized by surfactants (Tween 80, lecithin). - **Preparation:** High-pressure homogenization, microemulsion, solvent emulsification-evaporation. - **Advantages:** Improved physical stability, sustained release, scalable production, biocompatible. - **Limitation:** Drug expulsion during storage due to lipid polymorphic transitions. **4. Nanostructured Lipid Carriers (NLC) (1 mark):** - **Structure:** Blend of solid lipid + liquid lipid (oil) creating an **imperfect crystal lattice** with more voids for drug accommodation. - **Advantages:** Higher drug loading (~30–40% vs ~10% in SLN), reduced drug expulsion, prolonged release. **5. Summary comparison (1 mark):** - Liposomes → bilayer vesicles; SLN → solid matrix; NLC → imperfect solid + liquid lipid blend — collectively form the lipid nano carrier family with progressively improved drug loading and stability. --- #### Model Solution — (b) EPR Effect, Passive vs. Active Targeting, PEGylation (7 marks) **1. EPR Effect (2 marks):** - Tumors exhibit **leaky vasculature** (200–2000 nm pore size) due to VEGF-driven abnormal angiogenesis. - Tumor interstitium lacks functional lymphatic drainage. - Result: Macromolecules and **nano carriers (10–200 nm) accumulate preferentially** in tumor tissue. - First reported by **Matsumura & Maeda (1986)**, forms the cornerstone of **passive targeting**. **2. Passive vs. Active Targeting (3 marks):** | Aspect | Passive Targeting | Active Targeting | |--------|-------------------|------------------| | Mechanism | Exploits EPR effect | Ligand-receptor recognition | | Requirements | Nano carrier size 10–200 nm | Surface functionalization with ligand | | Examples | Doxil® accumulates in tumors | Folic acid-conjugated NPs target folate-receptor⁺ tumors | | Specificity | Moderate | High | | Limitations | Works mainly in solid tumors | Heterogeneous receptor expression | **3. PEGylation (2 marks):** - **Process:** Covalent attachment of **polyethylene glycol (PEG)** chains to the nano carrier surface. - **Stealth effect:** Creates a **hydrophilic, neutral corona** that reduces opsonization (adsorption of serum proteins) → evades **RES (reticuloendothelial system)** clearance. - **Consequence:** Prolonged **blood circulation half-life** (e.g., Doxil® circulates for ~55 hours vs ~5 min for unPEGylated doxorubicin). - **Trade-off:** PEGylation can reduce cellular uptake — "**PEG dilemma**" — addressed by cleavable PEG linkers. --- ### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the **polymer-based nano carriers** — nanospheres, nanocapsules, and dendrimers. Compare their structural features and drug-loading mechanisms. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With suitable derivations and examples, explain the **Higuchi and Korsmeyer–Peppas models** for drug release from nano carrier matrices. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — (a) Polymer-Based Nano Carriers (7 marks) **1. Introduction (1 mark):** Polymer-based nano carriers use biodegradable/biocompatible polymers (PLGA, PLA, chitosan, albumin, gelatin) to encapsulate/conjugate therapeutic agents. They offer tunable release kinetics and high stability. **2. Nanospheres (2 marks):** - **Structure:** Solid **matrix-type** colloidal particles where the drug is **dispersed uniformly throughout the polymer matrix**. - **Preparation:** Emulsification-solvent evaporation, nanoprecipitation, salting-out. - **Drug loading mechanism:** Drug is mixed with polymer during preparation → entrapped within the matrix. - **Release:** Diffusion through matrix + surface/bulk erosion of polymer. - **Example:** PLGA nanospheres of leuprolide (prostate cancer). **3. Nanocapsules (2 marks):** - **Structure:** **Core-shell architecture** — a polymeric shell surrounds an inner cavity (liquid or solid) where the drug is confined as a **reservoir**. - **Preparation:** Interfacial polymerization, interfacial deposition. - **Drug loading mechanism:** Drug is dissolved/dispersed in the core liquid (often oil) before shell formation → typically higher EE% than nanospheres. - **Release:** Diffusion through the polymer shell (Fickian) and shell degradation. **4. Dendrimers (2 marks):** - **Structure:** Highly **branched, monodisperse, tree-like macromolecules** with a central core, repetitive interior branching units, and surface terminal groups. Built generation by generation (G0, G1, G2 ... G10). - **Examples:** PAMAM (polyamidoamine), PPI (polypropylenimine). - **Drug loading mechanisms:** - **Encapsulation** in internal hydrophobic cavities (host-guest chemistry). - **Conjugation** to surface terminal groups (–NH₂, –COOH) via covalent linkers. - **Applications:** Cancer drug delivery, gene delivery (positively charged dendrimers complex with DNA), MRI contrast agents (Gd-DTPA dendrimer). **5. Comparison summary (0 mark — integrated above):** The three differ fundamentally: nanospheres = matrix-dispersed, nanocapsules = reservoir-coreshell, dendrimers = branched molecular architecture — each offers unique advantages for different drug chemistries. --- #### Model Solution — (b) Drug Release Models (7 marks) **1. Higuchi Model — Derivation & Application (4 marks):** **Assumptions:** - Drug is uniformly dispersed in an insoluble, non-swelling matrix. - Drug concentration $C_0 \gg C_s$ (saturation solubility). - Pseudo-steady-state diffusion through a depleted boundary layer. - Planar geometry, sink conditions. **Derivation (using Fick's second law):** Starting with: $$ \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2} $$ Applying the pseudo-steady-state approximation and integrating the mass balance, the cumulative drug released per unit area is: $$ Q = \sqrt{D \left( 2C_0 - C_s \right) C_s \cdot t} $$ In normalized form (most-quoted expression): $$ \dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = K_H \sqrt{t} $$ where $K_H$ is the Higuchi dissolution constant. The **square-root-of-time dependence** is the **signature** of Higuchi matrix-diffusion-controlled release. **Worked numerical example:** A nano carrier releases 60% of its drug in 4 h per Higuchi kinetics. Find release at 16 h. $$ K_H = \dfrac{0.60}{\sqrt{4}} = 0.30 $$ At $t = 16$ h: $$ \dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{16} = 1.20 \quad (\text{capped at } 100\%) $$ **Limitation:** Valid only for $M_t / M_\infty \leq 0.60$; fails for swellable or erodible matrices. --- **2. Korsmeyer–Peppas Model (3 marks):** **Equation:** $$ \dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = k \cdot t^n $$ where $k$ is the kinetic constant and $n$ is the **release exponent** that characterizes the mechanism. **Mechanism interpretation (for spherical geometry):** | Exponent $n$ | Release Mechanism | |--------------|-------------------| | $n \leq 0.43$ | Quasi-Fickian diffusion | | $0.43 < n < 0.85$ | Anomalous (non-Fickian) transport | | $n = 0.85$ | Case II transport (polymer relaxation-controlled) | | $n > 0.85$ | Super Case II transport | **Advantage over Higuchi:** The Peppas model captures **non-Fickian** transport (e.g., polymer chain relaxation, swelling, erosion) and is valid for the **first 60% of release**. **Worked numerical example:** A PLGA nano carrier released 30% in 2 h and 50% in 8 h. Determine $n$ and $k$. Taking logs: $$ \log(0.30) = \log(k) + n \log(2) \implies -0.523 = \log(k) + 0.301n $$ $$ \log(0.50) = \log(k) + n \log(8) \implies -0.301 = \log(k) + 0.903n $$ Subtracting: $$ -0.222 = -0.602n \implies n = 0.369 $$ Back-substituting: $$ \log(k) = -0.523 - 0.301(0.369) = -0.634 \implies k = 0.232 $$ Result: $n = 0.37$ → **Quasi-Fickian diffusion-dominated** release (drug diffuses faster than matrix relaxes). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Nano Carrier Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing nanospheres vs. nanocapsules:** Many students write both are "the same" or mix up their drug-location definitions. **Correct:** Nanospheres = matrix-dispersed; Nanocapsules = reservoir core-shell. (Loss: 1–2 marks.) > > 2. **Higuchi model applied beyond 60% release:** The Higuchi equation is valid **only up to $M_t/M_\infty \leq 0.60$**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if students use it for >60% release without noting the limitation. > > 3. **Forgetting units in EE% / DLC% calculations:** Both are **percentages (%)** — students sometimes omit the ×100 factor. (Loss: 1 mark.) > > 4. **Equating SLN and NLC:** NLC = SLN + liquid lipid (oil). NLC has **higher drug loading** and **less drug expulsion**. Examiners look for this distinction. > > 5. **Confusing PEGylation's role:** Some students think PEGylation "increases cellular uptake." **Correct:** PEGylation **decreases** non-specific uptake and prolongs circulation — this is the "stealth" effect. > > 6. **Wrong example drugs:** Writing "liposomes contain aspirin" or "gold nanoparticles deliver insulin" is factually wrong. Use canonical examples: **Doxil® (liposomal doxorubicin)**, **Onpattro® (lipid NP-siRNA)**, **Abraxane® (albumin-paclitaxel)**. > > 7. **Skipping the EPR mechanism:** When asked about tumor targeting, students often say "EPR effect" without explaining **leaky vasculature + poor lymphatic drainage**. Examiners want both components for full credit. > > 8. **Generation number for dendrimers:** Dendrimers grow in **discrete generations** (G0, G1, G2...) and are **monodisperse** — students often confuse them with random branched polymers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Nano Carriers** - [x] **Definition:** Engineered nanoscale vehicles (1–1000 nm, optimal 10–200 nm) for therapeutic/diagnostic delivery. - [x] **Five classes:** Lipid, Polymer, Inorganic, Viral, Hybrid. - [x] **Liposome architecture:** Bilayer vesicle, hydrophilic core + hydrophobic membrane. - [x] **SLN vs. NLC:** NLC adds a liquid lipid → higher drug loading, less expulsion. - [x] **Nanospheres = matrix-type; Nanocapsules = core-shell reservoir.** - [x] **Dendrimers:** Branched, monodisperse, generations G0–G10 (e.g., PAMAM). - [x] **Polymeric micelles:** Form above CMC; hydrophobic core + hydrophilic corona. - [x] **AuNPs:** Tunable plasmonics, thiol (–SH) surface chemistry. - [x] **SPIONs:** Fe₃O₄/γ-Fe₂O₃, superparamagnetic, MRI + hyperthermia. - [x] **MSNs:** High surface area, tunable pores, gatekeeper systems. - [x] **VLPs:** Non-infectious protein cages (HPV, HepB vaccines). - [x] **EPR Effect:** Leaky vasculature + poor lymphatic drainage → nano carriers accumulate in tumors (Matsumura & Maeda, 1986). - [x] **Passive vs. Active Targeting:** EPR-based vs. ligand-receptor-based. - [x] **PEGylation:** Stealth coating → evades RES → prolongs circulation. - [x] **Stimuli-responsive release:** pH, temperature, enzyme, magnetic field, light, redox. - [x] **Key clinical examples:** Doxil® (cancer), Onpattro® (siRNA), Abraxane® (albumin-bound paclitaxel), mRNA-LNP COVID vaccines. - [x] **EE% formula:** $(W_{loaded} / W_{total}) \times 100$. - [x] **DLC% formula:** $(W_{loaded} / W_{carrier}) \times 100$. - [x] **Stokes–Einstein equation:** $D_h = k_B T / (3 \pi \eta D_t)$. - [x] **Higuchi model:** $M_t / M_\infty = K_H \sqrt{t}$ (valid up to 60% release). - [x] **Korsmeyer–Peppas model:** $M_t / M_\infty = k t^n$ — exponent $n$ reveals mechanism. - [x] **Acceptable nano carrier PDI:** < 0.3 for mono-disperse. - [x] **Acceptable zeta potential magnitude:** $\vert \zeta \vert > 30$ mV for colloidal stability. - [x] **Surface-to-volume ratio of sphere:** $A/V = 3/r$ — drives nano-scale reactivity. - [x] **"Magic Bullet" concept:** Paul Ehrlich (1909) — modern embodiment = targeted nano carriers. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** Always **draw a labelled diagram** when explaining any nano carrier type in your KTU answer. Examiners award up to **1–2 extra marks** for clear, well-labelled schematics (especially for liposomes, dendrimers, and the EPR effect).
